coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots for effective corrosion inhibition of Q235 steel in concentrated sulphuric acid solution
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Xumin
Wei, Hongyu
Liu, Mingguang
zhou, Laishui
Shen, Guozhu
Li, Qin
Hussain, Ghulam
Yang, Fan
Fathi, Reham
Chen, Haiji
Ostrikov, Kostya (Ken)
coredata.dc:description: In recent years, steel corrosion, an issue affecting a trillion-dollar industry, is being addressed with nanotechnology technologies. Small clusters and nanoparticles, as well as numerous molecules, are viable solutions, with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) being among the best and most promising. However, the complexity and duration of the CQDs purification process, as well as a few other issues, limit their practical applications as corrosion inhibitors. Therefore, in this study, a new type of nitrogen-doped CQDs (N-CQDs) with an average size of 2.5 ± 0.8 nm were synthesized via hydrothermal reaction of amino-guanidine and citric acid using a robust purification process. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and potentiodynamic polarization (PDP) techniques were used to evaluate the new inhibitor's corrosion mitigation effect on Q235 steel in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution. X-ray photoelectronic spectroscopy (XPS) and FTIR analyses were employed for the chemical composition investigation of as-prepared N-CQDs and surface characterization of Q235 steel samples. The analysis of the impedance module and the current density of Q235 steel in the corrosive solution validates the effective protective performance of the inhibitor. The results showed that the maximum inhibition efficiency of 95.3% was achieved at the concentration of 200 mg/L. Moreover, it was found that the formation of the protective layer on the steel samples results from the Physico-chemical adsorption of the inhibitor on the steel surface and follows the Langmuir adsorption isotherm that was also confirmed via XPS. The quantum chemical calculations show that CQD nitrogen doping has an effect on corrosion inhibition performance. Overall, the corrosion inhibition efficiency of the carbon dots inhibitor is higher at the same concentration than that of most other similar inhibitor types.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-doped carbon dots
Q235 steel
Corrosion inhibitor
Quantum chemical calculations

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities , China [Grant No. NS2015055 and No. NP2020413 ]; the Administration of the Ministries and Commissions of the Central Government , China [Grant No. 011951G19061 ]; the National Natural Science Foundation of China , China [Grant No. 51105202 ]; the State Administration of PR China and Ministry of Education, China [Grant No. B16024].

This work was supported by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities, China [Grant No.NS2015055 and No.NP2020413]; the Administration of the Ministries and Commissions of the Central Government, China [Grant No. 011951G19061]; the National Natural Science Foundation of China, China [Grant No.51105202]; the State Administration of PR China and Ministry of Education, China [Grant No. B16024].

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM (insert HRTEM) image and particle size of CQDs.
UV–vis, FTIR and XPS spectra of N-CQDs (a) UV–vis; (b) FTIR; (c) XPS-full spectra; (d)XPS-C1s; (e) XPS-N1s; (f) XPS-O1s.
Corrosion rate (a) and inhibition efficacy (b) of Q235 steel in 0.5 M H2SO4 solutions at different exposure time at 298 K.
Corrosion rate (a) and inhibition efficiency (b) of Q235 steel in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution at different N-CQDs concentrations and temperatures after 24 h immersion time.
Arrhenius plots for Q235 steel in 0.5 M H2SO4 for varying concentrations of N-CQDs.
PDP plots for Q235 steel in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution with varying concentrations of N-CQDs at 298 K.
EIS results of Q235 steel in 0.5 H2So4 containing varying concentrations of N-CQDs after 24 h immersion time: (a) Nyquist image, (b, c) Bode image, (d) Equivalent circuit model.
SEM image of mild steel: (a) in absence of inhibitor, (b) in the presence of 200 mg/L N-CQDs.
AFM image of Q235 steel immersed in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution in the absence (a) and presence (b) of 200 mg/L N-CQDs.
XPS image of mild steel: (a) full spectrum, (b) C1s-N-CQDs, (c) Fe2p-Blank, (d) Fe2p-N-CQDs, (e) O1s-Blank, (f) O1s-N-CQDs, (g) N1s-Blank, (h) N1s-N-CQDs.
Langmuir adsorption isotherms at (a)298 K; (b) 308 K; (c) 318 K for N-CQDs adsorption on the Q235 steel surface.
The optimized structure and HOMO/LUMO distribution of inhibitors.
Thermodynamic parameters of the Q235 steel dissolution in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution at varying concentrations of N-CQDs.
Inhibition effect of different inhibitors on Q235 steel in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution at 298 K after 24 h immersion time.
PDP Parameters for Q235 steel in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution containing varying concentrations of N-CQDs at 298 K.
EIS parameters for Q235 steel electrode in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution containing different concentrations of N-CQDs at 298 K.
The Langmuir adsorption isotherm parameters of N-CQDs on Q235 steel surface at varying temperatures.
The quantum chemical parameters of N-CQDs based on the DFT/B3LYP/6–31 G++ method in the aqueous phase.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots for effective corrosion inhibition of Q235 steel in concentrated sulphuric acid solution

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Physics and Centre for Materials Science, Queensland University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Physics and Centre for Materials Science, Queensland University of Technology, Brisbane QLD 4000, Australia.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In recent years, steel corrosion, an issue affecting a trillion-dollar industry, is being addressed with nanotechnology technologies. Small clusters and nanoparticles, as well as numerous molecules, are viable solutions, with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) being among the best and most promising. However, the complexity and duration of the CQDs purification process, as well as a few other issues, limit their practical applications as corrosion inhibitors. Therefore, in this study, a new type of nitrogen-doped CQDs (N-CQDs) with an average size of 2.5 ± 0.8 nm were synthesized via hydrothermal reaction of amino-guanidine and citric acid using a robust purification process. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and potentiodynamic polarization (PDP) techniques were used to evaluate the new inhibitor's corrosion mitigation effect on Q235 steel in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution. X-ray photoelectronic spectroscopy (XPS) and FTIR analyses were employed for the chemical composition investigation of as-prepared N-CQDs and surface characterization of Q235 steel samples. The analysis of the impedance module and the current density of Q235 steel in the corrosive solution validates the effective protective performance of the inhibitor. The results showed that the maximum inhibition efficiency of 95.3% was achieved at the concentration of 200 mg/L. Moreover, it was found that the formation of the protective layer on the steel samples results from the Physico-chemical adsorption of the inhibitor on the steel surface and follows the Langmuir adsorption isotherm that was also confirmed via XPS. The quantum chemical calculations show that CQD nitrogen doping has an effect on corrosion inhibition performance. Overall, the corrosion inhibition efficiency of the carbon dots inhibitor is higher at the same concentration than that of most other similar inhibitor types.

ce:section.ce:para: Metal corrosion has always been a critical problem for many industrial applications over the past decades because it degrades product performance and life and results in significant direct and indirect economic losses[1–3]. The use of corrosion inhibitors is one of the most widely utilized ways of controlling corrosion mechanisms. For many years, traditional corrosion inhibitors such as chromates, phosphates, and nitrates were employed as efficient corrosion inhibitors. However, due to their high volatility, they emit hazardous gases that harm the environment[4–6]. With increased environmental consciousness, the use of eco-friendly chemical materials as corrosion inhibitors is gaining attention from researchers interested in this field[7]. As a result, the development of green inhibitors is now one of the most prominent study directions in the area of corrosion engineering[8–10].

Many different types of organic inhibitors have shown encouraging results as environmentally acceptable corrosion inhibitors for metallic alloys in various harsh environments[11]. These inhibitors' corrosion inhibition capacity originates from their active adsorption on the metal surface and the creation of a protective layer, which protects the metal dissolution in the corrosive solution[12–14]. The adsorption effectiveness of these organic inhibitors on the metal surface in the corrosive solution heavily influences the interfacial metal/solution properties such as double-layer capacitance and the kinetic of surface electron transfer phenomena. As a result, one of the most critical topics in surface science is the link between inhibitor adsorption and corrosion inhibition efficacy. Moreover, because of the importance and complexity of corrosion inhibition mechanisms of organic inhibitors, there is a growing interest in producing and modifying organic compounds as eco-friendly corrosion inhibitors.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are spherical, discrete, and small carbon nanoparticles (less than 10 nm in diameter). The remarkable optical properties and facilitated preparation process result in the extensive application of CQDs for bioimaging, biosensors, and catalysis. Likewise, the low toxicity, good biocompatibility, and large surface functional groups leading CQDs to be favorable candidates as corrosion inhibitors[15–19]. In this regard, Cui et al.[20] synthesized N-CQDs via 4-Aminosalicylic acid as corrosion inhibitor, and the results showed that the addition of N-CQDs significantly inhibits corrosion of Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution. In another study, Ye et al. synthesized several N-CQDs through different methods using ammonium citrate[21], citric acid and L-histidine[22], respectively. All the synthesized inhibitors presented superior results to control the mild steel corrosion in a 1 M HCl corrosive environment. In another related work, Cen et al.[23] also studied the corrosion inhibition of N, S-CQDs synthesized by aminosalicylic and thiourea through the hydrothermal method. The as-prepared N, S-CQDs showed a high capability to control the aluminum alloy in the acidic solution. Recently, Zhang et al.[24] also prepared the N, S-CQDs by O-phenylenediamines and solvothermal method. The results showed that the N, S-CQDs displayed excellent effectiveness for copper in sulfuric acid solution.

At present, there are still some areas that require substantial improvements: 1)The corrosion inhibition of N-CQDs on carbon steel in the sulfuric acid environment is worth further studying; 2) The preparation of CQDs for corrosion inhibition applications mainly relies on purification processes such as dialysis, which significantly increases the preparation time and economic cost; 3) More assessments are required to elucidate the influence of the process parameters and modification on the inhibition performance of this new and eco-benign compound.

Thus, a new type of N-CQD was synthesized for the first time in this study using a hydrothermal approach with aminoguanidine hydrochloride and citric acid as precursors and a simple purification process. Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) spectroscopy, X-ray Photo-electronic Spectroscopy (XPS), and Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) were used to investigate the chemical structure and compound of as-prepared N-CQDs[25]. The corrosion inhibition performance of inhibitors on Q235 steel in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution at various temperatures and concentrations was evaluated using gravimetric and electrochemical techniques. Furthermore, quantum chemical calculations were performed to understand better N-doped inhibition mechanisms and the influence of molecular structure and N atoms on inhibitor corrosion mitigation capability. Finally, the expected findings in this paper will provide a new perspective for the process simplification and corrosion inhibition mechanism of N-CQDs inhibitors in practical industrial applications, especially in the field of acid pickling.

In this study, aminoguanidine hydrochloride and citric acid were supplied from Aladdin Industrial Corporation. While, AR-grade 98 wt% sulfuric acid, ethyl alcohol, acetone were provided from Sinopharm Group. The Q235 steel samples with dimensions of 15 mm × 75 mm x 2 mm and 10 mm × 10 mm x 2 mm were prepared from a local supplier for weight loss and electrochemical analysis, respectively. Prior to tests, the samples were polished with various grids of emery papers (240 #, 400 #, 800 #, and 1200 #). They were cleaned and rinsed with water and acetone before being cold-blasted and stored in the desiccator for the upcoming investigations.

The N-CQDs were synthesized following the procedure from Gil et al.[26] with some modifications. 1.5 g of aminoguanidine hydrochloride and 1.5 g of citric acid were dissolved in 8 mL of deionized under ultra-sonication for 30 min. The solution was then heated in an autoclave for 4 h at 170 °C. After the reaction was completed, the resultant mixture was allowed to cool down to room temperature. Finally, the dark brown product was obtained, indicating the formation of carbon dots. Then the solution was purified through the centrifuge with high-speed rotation. To dry the obtained substance, a vacuum oven with a temperature of 80 °C and a time of 12 h was chosen. The innovative aspect of this modified process is the simplification of the purification process that can reduce the processing time and increase the functional group density of the N-CQDs.

A high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM), Tecnai G2 F30 S-twin model, was used to examine the particle size and surface morphology of samples. The chemical structure and constituents were investigated using X-ray photoelectronic spectroscopy (XPS) (Axis Ultra DLD), UV-Vis spectrometer (UV3600), and FTIR (IRPrestige-21) analysis.

After the initial weight measurement of the specimens, they were immersed into 0.5 M H2SO4 solution containing varying concentrations of N-CQDs for different durations(i.e., 4 h, 8 h, 16 h, 24 h, 36 h, 48 h, 60 h) at 298 K, 308 K, and 318 K, respectively). The Q235 steel samples were then washed by DI water and acetone, dried in the air, and reweighed[23]. The corrosion rate (
V
corr
) can be obtained from the Eq. (1):
(1)
V
c o r r
=
Δ W
A t
where ∆W is the weight loss, A is the exposed area of the metal sample, and t is the immersion time. The inhibition efficiency (η) and surface coverage (θ) could be defined by the following equation:
(2)
η ( % ) =
V
c o r r
0
−
V
c o r r
V
c o r r
0
× 100 %
(3)
θ =
V
c o r r
0
−
V
c o r r
V
c o r r
0
where
V
corr
and
V
corr
0
are the steel dissolution rate, with and without the presence of N-CQDs, respectively. In this study, results were measured three times, and averaged values were considered.

The potentiodynamic polarization (PDP) and Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) tests were carried out via a CHI660C electrochemistry workstation. The device had three electrodes: Q235 steel as the working electrode, saturated calomel electrode (SCE), and platinum plate as the reference and counter electrodes, respectively.

After establishing a steady-state of the open circuit potential (OCP) (30 min), the PDP analysis was performed in the pre-regulated potential range (i.e., +250 to −250 mV) with the scan rate of 1 mV/s.
For the EIS measurements, AC signals of five mV amplitude with the frequency spectrum from 100 kHz to 0.01 Hz were used[27]. The data analysis was utilized via Zview software.

The surface morphology and roughness of Q235 steel samples were investigated using AFM (MFP-30), SEM (Nova Nano SEM 450), and XPS (AXIS ULTRADLD) analyses. Before the tests, the specimens were immersed in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution with and without the 200 mg/L N-CQDs for 24 h at 298 K. After specific exposure time with the corrosive environment, the specimens were cleaned with distilled water, acetone and then dried[28].

Quantum chemical calculations of N-CQDs and precursors were carried out in aqueous phases using Gaussian 09 W software according to the density functional theory (DFT) (B3LYP method, 6–311 G++ (d, p))[29–31]. To correlate the molecular structure of the inhibitor and its corrosion mitigation performance, a series of electronic properties including HOMO and LUMO energies (EHOMO, ELUMO), the energy gap between the HOMO and LUMO (∆E), electronegativity (χ), chemical hardness (η), softness (σ), dipole moment (μ) and the fraction of the transferred electrons (∆N) were calculated using the following equations:
(4)
Δ E =
E
L U M O
−
E
H O M O
(5)
η =
1 2
(
E
L U M O
−
E
H O M O
)
(6)
σ =
1 η
(7)
χ = −
1 2
(
E
L U M O
−
E
H O M O
)
(8)
Δ N =
7 −
χ
i n h
2
η
inh
where, χ
inh and η
inh is the electronegativity and the hardness of the N-CQDs inhibitor, respectively[32].

Fig. 1 presents the morphology and distribution of N-CQDs, analyzed with a high-resolution TEM equipped with a particle size analyzer. The results reveal a uniform dispersion of spherical nanoparticles with an average size of 2.5 ± 0.8 nm. Moreover, the interplanar distance of 0.34 nm in the lattice fringes validates the high crystalline structure of as-prepared N-CQDs.

Fig. 2(a–f) shows the analysis of the obtained results of UV–vis, FTIR, and XPS spectra, respectively. An absorption peak, around 330 nm in the UV–vis spectra (Fig. 2(a)), can endorse the presence of CO and CN bonds[33]. The FTIR spectrum of N-CQDs is presented in Fig. 2(b). The adsorption peak of N-CQDs at 1213, 1436, 1636, 1686, 2275, 3341 cm−1 can be attributed to C–N, C–H, CN, CO, CC, and N-H/ OH bond, respectively. The presence of CC, C–N, and CN bonds indicates the successful transition of precursors into N-CQDs. XPS analysis was also used for a better understanding of product characteristics. The original XPS spectrum of N-CQDs is represented in Fig. 2(c). The chemical composition of N-CQDs is inferred to be 58.53 at% C, 19.24 at% N, and 22.22 at% O. XPSPEAK software were used to obtain the high-resolution XPS spectra (C 1s, N 1s), and the results are shown in Fig. 2. (e–f). The presence of four peaks in the C1s spectrum (Fig. 2e) at 284.7, 285.0, 286.9, and 288.9 eV are associated with CC, C–N, C–O/CN, and CO bonds, respectively. Likewise, Fig. 2(e) demonstrates three peaks for the N 1s spectrum. The peaks at 399.5, 400.2 and 401.1 eV are ascribed to the N–H, N–C and NC bonds which also corresponding to pyridine-like N, pyrrole-like N, and graphitic N, respectively. Generally, the amount of different types of nitrogen group has a great impact on the corrosion inhibition performance of N-CQDs. The inhibition efficiency of inhibitor will increase with the amount of pyrrole-like N and pyridine-like N increase and with the amount of graphitic N decrease[21].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of concentration and immersion time
ce:section.ce:para: Gravimetric measurement is a simple and dependable approach for determining the effect of inhibitor concentration, immersion time, and temperature on inhibitor inhibition efficacy. The corrosion rate and inhibition efficiency with different inhibitor concentrations and immersion times are presented in Fig. 3(a, b). With the increase of inhibitor concentration and immersion time, the corrosion rate decreases while the corrosion inhibition efficiency increases. Furthermore, the corrosion rate and inhibition efficiency show a sharp downward and rising trend in the first eight hours followed by a variation. This may be due to the rapid adsorption of corrosion inhibitors on the metal surface initially, and then the adsorption speed slows down. The highest efficiency of 95.3% is achieved at a concentration of 200 mg/L after 60 h immersion time.

Fig. 4(a, b) show the influence of temperature (i.e., 298–318 K) on the corrosion rate of Q235 steel and inhibition efficiency (η) for different concentrations of N-CQDs, after 24 h immersion time. It is to see that η slightly decreases with the increase of the temperature. The reduction in η at higher temperatures may result from promoting the kinetics of corrosion mechanisms for the un-inhibited metal surface along with the decomposition of the formed protective layer[34,35]. The influence of temperature on the corrosion rate can be explicated by activation energy (Ea ) derived from the Arrhenius equation[11]:
(9)
log
(
C
R
)
=
log
A −
E
a
2.303 R T
where A, T, and R denote the exponential constant, the media temperature, and the gas constant, respectively.

E
a is estimated from the slope of Arrhenius plots ( Fig. 5a), and the results are tabulated in Table 1. It is inferred that with the presence of the inhibitor, the value of E
a is substantially higher than the uninhibited solution. The larger value of E
a implies the enhancement of the energy barrier of corrosion reactions with the presence of N-CQDs, thereby making the corrosion more difficult to occur.

The derivative of the Arrhenius equation can be drawn to estimate the enthalpy (∆H
a ) and entropy (∆S
a ) of the systems (Eq. (10)), as follows:
(10)
ln
C
R
T
= −
Δ
H
a
R T
+
Δ
S
a
R
+
ln
(
R
N h
)
where N and h are Avogadro's number and Plank’s constant, respectively.

The values of ∆H
a and ∆S
a are presented in.

Table 1. The positive ∆H
a validates the endothermic formation of the activated complex. Likewise, the increase in negativity of ∆S
a by increasing the inhibitor’s concentration is attributed to the reduction in the disordering of the system during the reactant to the activated transition of the complexes. This ascribed the formation of a protective layer on the metal surface [36].

A small fraction of the precursor material will be retained in the final product due to incomplete precursor conversion in the hydrothermal reaction. As a result, precursor materials have a significant impact on corrosion inhibition performance. Table 2 compares the corrosion inhibition performance of precursor materials and synthesized N-CQDs as inhibitors, with the inhibitor added at a concentration of 200 mg/L. It can be seen that when AG and CA are used as corrosion inhibitors alone, their corrosion inhibition efficiency is very low, which can be almost ignored. It indicates that the precursor has little effect on the corrosion inhibition performance of N-CQDs, and the corrosion inhibition performance of the final product mainly comes from N-CQDs.

Fig. 6 shows the potentiodynamic polarization curve of Q235 steel in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution with different concentrations of N-CQDs at 298 K. The value of corrosion current density i
corr , corrosion potential E
corr , anode polarizability β
a , cathode polarizability β
c are listed in Table 3. The corrosion inhibition efficiency η and surface coverage θ for different concentrations can be calculated by the following equations:
(11)
θ =
i
c o r r
0
−
i
c o r r
i
c o r r
(12)
η =
i
c o r r
0
−
i
c o r r
i
c o r r
× 100 %
where
i
corr
0
and
i
corr
are corrosion current densities for un-inhibited and inhibited steels, respectively.

One can conclude that as the inhibitor concentration increases, the i
corr decreases, and the E
corr moves to the less negative values[37]. Generally, the corrosion current density is a kinetic parameter, which can reflect the speed of corrosion reaction, while the corrosion potential is a thermodynamic parameter determining the tendency of corrosion reactions to occur. Therefore, the transformation of i
corr and E
corr indicate that corrosion rate decreases with the increase of N-CQDs concentration. Likewise, there is a minor change in the anodic and cathodic polarizability slopes (i.e., β
a and β
c ), indicating that the N-CQDs are a mixed inhibitor type. It is suggested that the inhibitors can be adsorbed on the metal surface, thereby blocking the active sites on the metal surface. However, there is a negligible influence on the anode and cathode reactions [38,39]. The slight displacement of E
corr (i.e., 14 mV) also confirms that the N-CQDs are a mixed type inhibitor[40,41]. From the obtained current densities (Eq. (12)), the inhibition efficiency up to 91.4% can be achieved at the concentration of 200 mg/L that is consistent with the gravimetric results.

Fig. 7 shows the EIS results of Q235 steel in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution containing different concentrations of N-CQDs at 298 K. The obvious capacitive arcs in the Nyquist diagram (Fig. 7(a)) suggest that the charge transfer is the dominant mechanism to control the corrosion of Q235 steel with the presence of inhibitors. The diameter of the capacitive arc gradually increases by the increase of the inhibitor’s concentration endorsing that N-CQDs form an effective protective layer on the metal surface [42].

The Bode diagrams are presented in Fig. 7(b). The |Z|0.01 Hz value usually reflects the corrosion resistance of the system. It is inferred that the value of |Z|0.01 Hz is larger, and the corrosion rate will be lower. With the increase of the inhibitor concentration, the value of |Z|0.01 Hz increases from 10.35 Ω∙cm2 to 185.92 Ω∙cm2 that implies the reduction in the corrosion rate. Fig. 7(c) shows the Bode phase angle curves. All the curves are time constants, and the peak height increases with the increase of N-CQDs concentration, indicating that N-CQDs have a potent affinity to be adsorbed at the metal/ solution interface [43]. The results show that N-CQDs indeed inhibit the corrosion process of Q235 steel, yet it does not change the corrosion mechanisms, which is consistent with the results obtained from potentiodynamic polarization analysis.

The equivalent circuit model is shown in Fig. 7(d), and the parameters are fitted by Zview software, and the results are presented in Table 4. Among them, R
s is the solution resistance, R
p denotes the polarization resistance. CPE is the constant phase angle element, representing the capacitive property of the electric double layer, which could be defined as[44]:
(13)
Z
C P E
=
1
Y
0
(
j ω
)
n
where n is the exponent, Y
0 is the CPE constant, and j and ω are the imaginary number and the angular frequency, respectively.

The magnitude of n can be used to depict the CPE as capacitance (n = 1), Warburg impedance (n = 0.5), resistance (n = 0), and inductance (n = −1)[11]. The corrosion inhibition efficiency (η) and double-layer capacitance (C
dl ) can be estimated using the following expressions[45]:
(14)
C
d l
=
Y
0
(
ω
max
)
n − 1
(15)
η
( % )
=
R
P
−
R
P
0
R
P
× 100 %
where ωmax represents the frequency for the maximum imaginary part of the impedance (rads/s).
R
p
and
R
p
0
denote the polarization resistances with the presence and absence of the inhibitors, respectively.

It can be inferred from Table 4 that the values of R
s show a minor variation under different conditions, indicating that the addition of N-CQDs does not change the properties of the acid solution. The value of R
p increases with the increase of the inhibitor concentration, promoting the formation of an effective protective film on the metal surface. Moreover, the variation of the electric double layer capacitance C
dl is opposite to that of the value of R
p
[1]. It is mainly because the N-CQDs inhibitor gradually substituted for the adsorbed water molecules on the steel surface. Adsorption of inhibitors results in a reduction in the exposed area of the steel surface. This may lead to the reduction in the dielectric constant and the increase of the electric double layer thickness, leading to the decrease of the electric double-layer capacitance. The inhibitor film formation can also be assessed by the Helmholtz model (Eq. (16)):
(16)
C
dl
=
ε
0
ε
d
S
where ε0 and ε are the permittivity of air and local dielectric constant of the film, d denotes the thickness of the electrical double layer, and S signifies the geometric surface area of the specimen in corrosive solution[42].

By the enhancement of the concentration of the inhibitor, a higher density of the inhibitors can be adsorbed on the metal surface leading to the decrease of the contact area (S) between the Q235 steel and the solution, which also results in a decline of the value of C
dl . The inhibition efficiency (η) increases with the increase of the inhibitor concentration and approach to 91.2%, at 200 mg/L, which is consistent with the potentiodynamic and gravimetric results.

The surface morphology of steel samples after 24 h exposure to 0.5 M H2SO4 with and without the presence of the inhibitor is presented in Fig. 8. The corrosion products of the un-inhibited specimen are visible while the inhibited steel poses a relatively smooth surface, and even the parallel stripes resulting from the abrasion treatment are noticeable. This observation supports the formation of a protective layer due to the presence of N-CQDs, thereby retarding the metal dissolution in the aggressive acidic solution[46].

AFM analysis was performed to further analyze the average surface roughness of Q235 steel after 24 h immersion in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution with and without the presence of N-CQDs. The AFM surface micrograph of the un-inhibited Q235 steel sample ( Fig. 9(a)) contains a considerable quantity of pits resulting from the intensified sulfuric acid attack. The corresponding height profile curve shows a peak at about 343.8 nm with an average roughness of 82.9 nm. However, the height profile curve of mild steel surface protected by the corrosion inhibitor (Fig. 9(b)) shows peak and valley at about 126.0 nm and the tangible reduction in the surface roughness (i.e., 34.9 nm). This analysis substantiates the acceptable effectiveness of N-CQDs to control the corrosion of Q235 steel corrosion in sulfuric acid solution [46].

XPS analysis was carried out to assess the composition and adsorption of the formed layer on the metal surface. Fig. 10(a) shows the full spectra of Q235 steel after 24 hr immersion in the acidic solution containing 200 mg/L of N-CQDs. The noteworthy increase in the nitrogen content on the metal surface suggests the adsorption of N-CQDs. Fig. 10 (b–h) shows the high-resolution spectra and the peak fitting of each element (i.e., Fe 2p, C 1s, O 1 s, N 1s) in the absence and presence of the inhibitor. There is no tangible alteration in Fe 2p spectrum for the inhibited and un-inhibited systems, indicating that N-CQDs did not participate in the corrosion reactions. As regards the C 1 s spectrum with the presence of inhibitors, three peaks at 284.8 eV, 286.4 eV, and 288.3 eV determine the CC, CO, and COOR bonds, respectively. The O ls spectrum is deconvoluted into two peaks. The peak at 531.8 eV is attributed to the CO bond, and the peak at 532.1 eV is associated with CO[47]. Likewise, two peaks of the N 1s spectrum at 399.4 eV and 401.0 eV are related to NC and NFe bonds, respectively. According to the above discussion, XPS analysis validates the adsorption of N-CQDs on the metal surface, thereby retarding the corrosion process [48].

The adsorption of inhibitors on the metal surface is a dynamic process that can be assessed by analyzing the adsorption isotherms[49]. It relates the corrosion inhibitor ion coverage on the electrode surface (θ) and the concentration (C) in the corrosive solution. The adsorption isotherm describes the interaction between inhibitors and metal surfaces at a specific temperature. The most common types of adsorption isotherms include Langmuir, Temkin, and Frumkin. Langmuir isotherm assumes that there is no interaction between the adsorbed inhibitors[23]. Temkin adsorption isotherm considers the effect of the heterogeneous adsorption activity on the adsorption-desorption equilibrium, while Frumkin isotherm cogitates the interaction between particles adsorbed on the metal surface. The three isotherms are acquired by the following equation:
(17)
Langmuir :
C θ
= C +
1
K
ads
(18)
Temkin :
log
θ C
=
log
K
a d s
− g θ
(19)
Frumkin :
log
θ
(
1 − θ
)
C
=
log
K
a d s
+ g θ
where C is the inhibitor concentration, θ is the inhibitor coverage on the metal surface, K
ads is the adsorption-desorption equilibrium constant abd g is the inhibitor interaction parameter.

From the fitting of weight loss data into the formula for fitting and the obtained regression coefficient (R
2 ), it follows that the Langmuir isotherm ( Fig. 11) provided the best fit as compared to the other isotherms (Fig. S1 and Fig. S2). The corresponding parameters of the Langmuir isotherm are tabulated in
Table 5. In general, the value of K
ads is attributed to the adsorption ability of the inhibitor such that the larger value of K
ads represents the higher adsorption ability of the inhibitor on the surface. The obtained quantities of K
ads at different temperatures show that the value of K
ads decreases by increasing the temperature. The results are in agreement with those obtained in weightlessness experiments.

The standard Gibbs free energy of the system (
∆
G
ads
0
) is another essential parameter to investigate the adsorption mechanism of inhibitors. The
∆
G
ads
0
values of −20 kJ/mol or less negative signify the physisorption process, while the
∆
G
ads
0
values of - 40 kJ/mol or higher negativity suggest the chemisorption process [50,51].
∆
G
ads
0
can be estimated by the following equation[52]:
(20)
Δ
G
a d s
0
= − R T
ln ( 1 ×
10
6
K
a d s
)
where R represents the molar gas constant and T is the absolute temperature.

The calculated values of
∆
G
ads
0
in the presence of N-CQDs are −25.88 kJ/mol, −26.84 kJ/mol, and −27.95 kJ/mol at 298 K, 308 K, and 318 K, respectively. These findings allow concluding that N-CQDs are adsorbed on the metal surface by chemical and physical adsorption.

The quantum chemical calculation is an effective strategy for a better understanding of the corrosion mitigation mechanism of inhibitors. In this part, the correlation between the molecular structure of the inhibitor and its reactivity is analyzed based on the dynamic functional theory (DFT), and the effect of N-doping on the inhibition performance is examined. Fig. 12 presents the optimized structures along with the lowest and highest occupied molecular orbital (LOMO/HOMO) of precursors, CQDs, and synthesized N-CQDs. Compared with CQDS, the frontier orbitals of N-CQDs are more widely and uniformly distributed on the whole molecular surface, indicating that N-CQDs feature the planar or quasi planar adsorption on the metal surface.

The key quantitative parameters of precursors, CQDs, and N-CQDs molecular structure are listed in Table 6. EHOMO represents the electron donation capability of the inhibitor to the metal surface. A higher EHOMO value indicates that the inhibitor has a greater ability to donate electrons, resulting in better corrosion inhibition performance. The smaller the value of EHOMO shows the higher ability of the inhibitor to accept the electrons from the outer d orbital of the metal to form the feedback bond[53]. The difference between the E
HOMO and E
LOMO (i.e., energy gap (ΔE)) is an important index of molecular stability[54]. The lower value of ΔE in Table 6 indicates the improved ability of N-CQDs for charge donation to the metal surface compared to undoped CQDs, thus leading to the higher inhibition efficiency of the modified quantum dots.

The dipole moment μ is an index of molecular polarity. The higher value of μ demonstrates the stronger dipole interaction of corrosion inhibitor and the metal surface leading to a more effective adsorption performance[55]. Because of the solvation effect, the protonation of inhibitors in the acidic medium may also promote the μ value [56]. Compared to the precursors and CQDs, N-CQDs have a notably higher value of µ (i.e., 12.54 D), supporting the higher adsorption affinity of N-CQDs on the metal surface. The electron transfer number (ΔN) is another essential parameter to validate the effectiveness of the electron transfer and the inhibition performance of the inhibitor [57]. The high value of ΔN (i.e., 1.03 e) can also confirm the higher electron donation ability of N-CQDs as compared to CQDs. The aforementioned analyses confirm that nitrogen doping altered the quantitative parameters (particularly the dipole moment) of CQDs' molecular structure and significantly improved their corrosion inhibition performance.

From electrochemical analysis, we can conclude that the N-CQDs inhibit the cathodic and anodic reactions of corrosion reaction simultaneously, thus reducing the corrosion rate of Q235 steel. Then, surface analysis and adsorption type analysis further reveals the adsorption film formed on the metal surface, which was attributed to chemical and physical adsorption between N-CQDs and steel surface in sulfuric acid solution. The chemical adsorption was attributed to the formation of coordination bonds due to the interaction between long pair electrons of unprotonated N atoms on N-CQDs surface and the unoccupied 3D orbitals outside the iron, which can be confirmed by the discovery of NFe bonds in XPS results. Moreover, physical adsorption was due to the electrostatic interaction between protonated N and residual charge on the steel surface. Besides, quantum chemical calculation analysis further proves that N-CQDs can effectively inhibit corrosion reactions. At the same time, the doped nitrogen atoms on CQDs play a significant role in corrosion inhibition, which also indirectly proves the existence of physical adsorption and chemical adsorption.

In this paper, a new eco-friendly corrosion inhibitor based on N-doped carbon dots (N-CQDs) has been proposed and characterized. The performance of the inhibitor has been evaluated to control corrosion of Q235 steel in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution. From this study, the following main conclusions are drawn:
(1)
The N-CQDs are well distributed with an average particle size of 2.5 ± 0.8 nm.
(2)
The N-CQDs appear as a mixed-type inhibitor as revealed by the gravimetric measurements and electrochemical analysis. Its inhibition efficiency is 95.3% (max), which was achieved at 200 mg/L of N-CQDs.
(3)
The inhibition mechanism of N-CQDs is based on the formation of an effective protective layer on the Q235 steel surface in acidic solution, as confirmed by SEM and AFM analyses.
(4)
The kinetic and thermodynamic analyses (i.e., K
ads and
∆
G
ads
0
)
suggest that the Langmuir adsorption isotherm for the newly developed N-CQDs. Further, the adsorption mechanism is controlled by the physical and chemical processes as revealed by the XPS analysis. These processes ensure the effective absorption of N-CQDs into the surface, thereby isolating them from the aggressive acidic medium.
(5)
The quantum chemical calculations show that N-doping has a significant impact on the corrosion inhibition effects of N-CQDs when compared to undoped CQDs.
